title: Cheap eats in Melbourne (without living on instant noodles)
summary: Save money without boring meals: build your under-$15 food routine near home, uni, or work.
content:
## Why this matters now
Food spending can quietly wreck your weekly budget. The goal is not to eat perfectly, it is to eat consistently without panic.

## Quick guide
- Pick one suburb base (home, uni, or work) and search there first.
- Save 5 reliable spots: one lunch, one dinner, one late option, one healthy option, one backup.
- Use lunch specials and student offers before dinner menus.
- Keep one low-cost home meal ready for days when delivery temptation hits.

## What to do this week
1. Open your map app and save 3 cheap options within 15 minutes.
2. Set a weekly food cap and track it once mid-week.
3. Add one emergency pantry meal at home.

## Near Me CTA
Find affordable options with Near Me pre-filtered to Food.

relevant_links.label: City of Melbourne: Free and cheap activities

title: Your first grocery run (what to buy, what to skip)
summary: A first-shop list that covers real meals, not random snacks and regret buys.
content:
## Why this matters now
Your first grocery trip sets your routine. Most people overspend because they buy ingredients with no meal plan.

## Quick guide
- Start with 2 proteins, 2 carbs, 3 vegetables, 2 breakfast items.
- Buy frozen vegetables for week-one flexibility.
- Choose ingredients that can be reused across at least 2 meals.
- Skip bulk snacks until your staples are covered.

## Starter basket
Eggs, rice or pasta, onions, garlic, frozen veg, bread, milk, fruit, one canned protein, one snack.

## What to do this week
1. Plan 5 dinners before shopping.
2. Shop once with a list and once for top-ups only.
3. Keep receipts for one week so you can adjust your budget.

title: 5 meals, 5 ingredients (for low-energy days)
summary: Simple repeatable meals for when you are busy, tired, or low on motivation.
content:
## Why this matters now
When life gets messy, simple meal systems beat ambitious cooking plans.

## Quick guide
Pick 5 ingredients and rotate them:
- Eggs
- Rice
- Frozen mixed vegetables
- Canned tuna or beans
- Pasta sauce

## 5 meal ideas
- Fried rice with egg and veg
- Tuna rice bowl
- Bean and veg pasta
- Omelette with toast
- Veg rice soup

## What to do this week
1. Batch-cook one base carb.
2. Prep one protein in advance.
3. Keep one 10-minute meal for stressful nights.

relevant_links.label: Moneysmart: How to do a budget

title: Melbourne transport 101 (myki without confusion)
summary: Understand myki, fare caps, and planning basics so daily travel is less stressful.
content:
## Why this matters now
Transport confusion costs time and money. Once you set your routine, commuting gets easier fast.

## Quick guide
- Use the official PTV journey planner for routes and disruptions.
- Learn daily fare caps so you can estimate weekly travel cost.
- Keep myki topped up before peak hours.
- Save your regular journeys in the app.

## What to do this week
1. Save home-to-uni/work route.
2. Save a backup route.
3. Check if you are eligible for concessions.

## Friendly reminder
The Free Tram Zone helps in the CBD, but only in marked areas.

title: How to get from A to B (when plans change)
summary: A practical backup plan for disruptions, late finishes, and route changes.
content:
## Why this matters now
Melbourne services change often. A backup plan keeps you calm and on time.

## Quick guide
- Check disruptions before leaving.
- Keep two route options: fastest and simplest.
- Add a 10-minute transfer buffer for unfamiliar trips.
- Screenshot directions if your signal is patchy.

## What to do this week
1. Build one late-night route home.
2. Save nearest stop/station details.
3. Share your travel ETA with a trusted person when late.

title: How to book your first GP (no awkward guesswork)
summary: A plain-English checklist for finding a clinic, asking the right questions, and booking confidently.
content:
## Why this matters now
You do not need to wait until things are bad to find a GP. Having one clinic ready saves panic later.

## Quick guide
- Search nearby clinics first, then filter by billing style.
- Ask 3 questions when calling: fee, earliest appointment, and new patient process.
- Bring Medicare card, ID, current medication list.
- If it is mental health related, ask about a GP mental health plan.

## Phone script
"Hi, I am a new patient. Do you have appointments this week, and what are your fees?"

## What to do this week
1. Save one GP near home and one near work/uni.
2. Book a non-urgent first consult if you can.

title: Bulk billing explained (in normal language)
summary: Understand bulk billing vs mixed billing so you can avoid surprise costs.
content:
## Why this matters now
Medical costs are stressful when money is tight. Knowing the billing model upfront helps you choose properly.

## Quick guide
- Bulk billed means Medicare covers the consultation cost for eligible patients.
- Mixed billing means some consults still have out-of-pocket fees.
- Always confirm billing before booking.
- Keep your Medicare details updated.

## Ask this at booking
"Will this appointment be bulk billed for me, and are there any gap fees?"

## What to do this week
1. Find 2 clinics and compare fee policies.
2. Save one affordable fallback clinic.

relevant_links.label: Services Australia concession and health care cards

title: GP vs urgent care vs emergency vs 000
summary: A simple triage guide for deciding where to go when you are unwell.
content:
## Why this matters now
When you are stressed or in pain, decisions feel harder. Use this as a simple rule-set.

## Quick guide
- GP: non-urgent care, ongoing issues, scripts, referrals.
- Nurse-on-Call: you are unsure and need advice now.
- VVED/Urgent pathways: same-day non-life-threatening support.
- 000: severe chest pain, trouble breathing, major bleeding, collapse, immediate danger.

## What to do this week
1. Save key health numbers in your phone.
2. Share this guide with your housemate so both of you know what to do.

title: Your first week living alone (survival checklist)
summary: A realistic week-one checklist to reduce stress and stop things spiralling.
content:
## Why this matters now
Week one is usually messy. A small system now prevents bigger stress later.

## Quick guide
- Set reminders: rent, bills, bin night, laundry.
- Build your local essentials map: supermarket, pharmacy, GP, transport stop.
- Keep emergency contacts visible.
- Do a 10-minute nightly reset (dishes, surfaces, tomorrow plan).

## Week-one checklist
- Utilities and internet confirmed
- Basic pantry stocked
- Medication and health contacts ready
- Travel route tested

## What to do this week
Pick 3 recurring reminders and set them today.

title: Share house survival (without drama)
summary: Set clear house rules early for bills, cleaning, guests, and boundaries.
content:
## Why this matters now
Most share house stress is communication, not personality. Clear agreements early save friendships later.

## Quick guide
- Agree on bills, payment dates, and split method.
- Set a basic cleaning rotation.
- Clarify guest rules and quiet hours.
- Use one group chat for house logistics only.

## Critical rental tip (Victoria)
If housemates change on a lease, update bond records correctly and quickly.

## What to do this week
1. Schedule a 20-minute house setup chat.
2. Write decisions in one shared note.
3. Confirm bond/lease names are up to date.

title: Free things to do when you are skint
summary: Keep your social life alive with free Melbourne options that still feel fun.
content:
## Why this matters now
When money is tight, isolation can creep in. Free plans help your mood without smashing your budget.

## Quick guide
- Use City of Melbourne free listings first.
- Plan around free events, markets, galleries, and walks.
- Pick social plans that do not require spending by default.

## What to do this week
1. Save 2 free events for this weekend.
2. Invite one friend to a low-cost plan (walk, market, gallery).

title: Feeling lonely? You are not the only one
summary: Low-pressure steps to reconnect when moving, studying, or working feels isolating.
content:
## Why this matters now
Big life transitions can feel lonely even when you are surrounded by people. This is common, and fixable with small actions.

## Quick guide
- Send one message first, even if it feels awkward.
- Join one recurring group (sport, club, class, volunteering).
- Swap 20 minutes of scrolling for one outdoor reset.

## What to do this week
1. Message one person to catch up.
2. Try one regular activity you can repeat next week.

## If it feels heavier than loneliness
Reach out to professional support early.

title: Crisis lines and free support (save before you need)
summary: A practical support list for difficult moments, including who to call and when.
content:
## Why this matters now
In a crisis, searching takes too long. Save support contacts now so help is one tap away.

## Quick guide
- Immediate danger: call 000.
- Emotional crisis: Lifeline.
- Suicide-specific counselling: Suicide Call Back Service.
- Youth support: headspace.

## Build your help note
Create a phone note called "Help Now" with services and one trusted personal contact.

## What to do this week
Pin your help note and share it with someone you trust.

title: Money reset when your account looks scary
summary: A 48-hour reset plan to regain control when spending feels out of hand.
content:
## Why this matters now
Money anxiety gets worse when ignored. A short reset gives you control fast.

## 48-hour reset
- Pause non-essential spending.
- List fixed costs and due dates.
- Plan 7 days of groceries and transport.
- Check concession eligibility.

## What to do this week
1. Do a 15-minute Sunday money check.
2. Move bill money into a separate account if possible.

title: Bad day emergency routine (10-minute reboot)
summary: A tiny action plan for overwhelmed days when normal routines feel impossible.
content:
## Why this matters now
On rough days, thinking about everything at once is exhausting. A short reset helps you restart.

## 10-minute reboot
1. Drink water.
2. Open blinds or step outside.
3. Pick one tiny task (shower, dishes, short walk).
4. Message one person.
5. Decide only the next step, not the whole week.

## If you feel unsafe
Call 000 for immediate danger, or use crisis support services now.
